purpose: The set-wide DJ-Search novelty *distribution* (spec 28, M1a). Where `originality_audit` scores ONE target against a pool, this scores a *corpus* against itself: for each document it runs DJ-Search reconstructibility against the rest of the corpus (leave-one-out), then reports the DISTRIBUTION of per-document originality ({min/p25/median/p75/max/mean/sd} + decile histogram) plus a per-document table and an ordered-pair mutual-reconstructibility census. Wraps the shipped `audit_originality` (clean-room DJ Search, arXiv:2410.04265); it does NOT re-implement the matcher. NO single "corpus originality score" headline (a lone scalar is a verdict in disguise — the distribution + table is the descriptive object). NOT an AI/human or plagiarism determination: low novelty = more reconstructible FROM THIS corpus, NOT 'AI' (a tight prompt, a shared genre, a single source, or a house style all lower it legitimately); measurements only, no band/verdict; the claim-license refuses provenance / plagiarism / selection.
use_when: You want to read how mutually reconstructible a CORPUS of documents is (the novelty distribution across the set), not just one target vs a pool
You're looking for a homogenized, mutually-reconstructible set vs a spread-out one
The corpus has >= --min-docs (default 3) documents, each >= ~24 words (>= min_ngram x 3)
do_not_use_when: You want an AI-provenance call (low novelty is NOT 'AI'; this is not an AI signal)
You want a plagiarism / derivative-work / copyright determination (it is a measurement)
The corpus is < --min-docs, a deliberately templated genre, or a single-source pool (overlap is expected and not diagnostic; reconstructibility is corpus- and register-dependent)

inputs.corpus: a --corpus-dir of texts OR a --manifest (JSONL; id + text|text_path)

compute.cost_note: stdlib only; no models. Deterministic; reuses the originality_audit substring index leave-one-out across the corpus.

examples.description: Novelty distribution across a corpus directory

references: DJ Search / Creativity Index — AI as Humanity's Salieri (arXiv:2410.04265): https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.04265
